local_names.malay: Susu kacang soya dengan mutiara tapioka

aliases: Susu kacang soya dengan mutiara tapioka

malay: Susu kacang soya dengan mutiara tapioka

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a soy milk entry; the tapioca pearl addition is not separately coded. Closest parent match used for the soy milk base component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers plain soya milk. The tapioca pearl component is not separately represented; closest parent match used for the soy milk base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for soy milk with tapioca pearls as a combined product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g (one cup), as commonly sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and soy milk beverage shops.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk with Pearls — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for soy milk with pearls (cr_id D02030011). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central soy milk reference (NDB 16222) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for soy milk (plain, unsweetened). The tapioca pearl component has a higher GI (~70–85 for cooked tapioca); however, the composite GI of 34 is retained from the HPB input record (confidence 0.6) as an analogous estimate for the soy milk base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 26.4) / 100 ≈ 9.0 for a 250 g serving (26.4 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.34 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage exceeds the AHA threshold of 5 g/100 g for sugar-sweetened beverages, independently elevating blood pressure risk despite very low sodium (13 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this beverage due to its sugar-sweetened status (7.34 g sugar/100 g); unsweetened plain soy milk without tapioca pearls is a clinically preferable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Combined effect of 7.34 g added sugar/100 g and 10.55 g total carbohydrate/100 g from tapioca pearls (high-GI refined starch) with only 0.8 g dietary fibre/100 g produces significant postprandial glucose excursions; qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage; the tapioca pearls are high-GI refined starch and the added sugar accelerates glucose absorption — unsweetened soy milk without pearls is the recommended substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage status (7.34 g sugar/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; fat content is low (1.63 g/100 g) and sodium is very low (13 mg/100 g), which are favourable, but the sugar burden remains a concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sweetened soy beverage due to its sugar load (7.34 g/100 g), which is linked to dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk; plain unsweetened soy milk is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tapioca pearls contribute refined starch and the added sugar (7.34 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; soy isoflavones in the milk base have mild anti-inflammatory properties but do not offset the refined carbohydrate burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage due to its refined starch and sugar content, which promote inflammation; plain unsweetened soy milk is a preferable anti-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soy milk with tapioca pearls is a popular sweetened plant-based beverage sold widely at Singaporean hawker centres, soy milk stalls, and bubble tea shops. It consists of freshly prepared or commercially produced soy milk — made by soaking, grinding, and boiling soybeans — combined with cooked chewy tapioca pearls (also called boba or sago pearls) and typically sweetened with sugar syrup. The beverage has a creamy, mildly beany flavour from the soy milk base, complemented by the soft, slightly chewy texture of the tapioca pearls. Per 100 g, it provides 72 kcal, 3.66 g protein, 1.63 g fat, 10.55 g carbohydrate, 7.34 g sugar, 0.8 g dietary fibre, 13 mg sodium, 164 mg calcium, and 0.63 mg iron. The calcium content is notably high due to calcium-fortified soy milk commonly used in Singapore. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (>5 g sugar/100 g) and is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. The tapioca pearls are a high-GI refined starch that accelerates postprandial glucose rise. The GI of the soy milk base is low (~34), but the composite product has a higher effective glycaemic impact due to the pearls. Unsweetened soy milk without pearls is the clinically recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is soy milk with pearls healthy?
Can diabetics drink soy milk with tapioca pearls?
How many calories in soy milk with pearls?
Is pearl soy milk suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of soy milk with boba?
Is soy milk with pearls vegan?
Is soy milk with tapioca pearls gluten free?
What is the GI of soy milk with pearls?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02030011) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central soy milk reference as HPB does not separately report this value. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain soy milk (GI 34), retained from the HPB input record.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.21 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for plain soy milk (NDB 16222, SR Legacy) as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this composite product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite soy milk with tapioca pearls product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI value of 34 is an analogous estimate based on plain soy milk from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite GI including tapioca pearls is expected to be higher in practice. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 250 g serving: (34 × 26.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 9.0. 4. Calcium value of 164 mg/100 g reflects calcium-fortified soy milk as commonly sold in Singapore. 5. The food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage per AHA criteria (>5 g sugar/100 g).
